items.q: What is the most common informal way to form a yes/no question in French?
items.correct: Rising intonation (statement said as a question) or using 'est-ce que'.
items.why: Students learn formal inversion first. In conversation, French uses intonation or est-ce que far more often. Inversion sounds formal/written - 'Tu parles français?' is the everyday form.

items.q: Which letter is typically SILENT at the end of French words like 'petit' or 'froid'?
items.why: Students try to pronounce every letter. Final consonants in French are usually silent (CaReFuL exceptions: c, r, f, l). Petit is pronounced 'puh-TEE', not 'puh-TEET'.

items.q: How do you describe weather in the past, e.g. 'it was sunny'?
items.misconception: Passé composé - 'Il a fait beau' (which suggests a completed event).
items.why: Weather descriptions in past narratives use IMPERFECT (ongoing state/description). Passé composé is for completed events. 'Quand je suis arrivé, il faisait beau' = ongoing weather backdrop; arrived = completed action.

items.q: What year was the Code Napoleon?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1804. “1789” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Avocat. “Juge” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Declaration des droits de l'homme adopted?
items.why: Exact answer: 1789. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
